episode.title: The instrument you keep — Files of Record
episode.description: A fictional episode used as a render-as-html fixture: durable instruments beat ephemeral apps when memory is the moat.
episode.bottom_line: When memory is the moat, durable instruments win over ephemeral apps.

takeaways: Treat the artifact as the file of record, not a preview layer.
Memory beats flow when the user revisits.
Round-trip editing is what makes a file an instrument.

claims.claim: Most consumer apps optimize for the next session; instruments optimize for the next year. The one that compounds wins.
claims.evidence: Notion's editable-document framing outlasted dozens of single-purpose flow apps that launched the same year.

claims.claim: An instrument's bar is that the output is editable in the same surface where it was produced — without re-uploading or re-prompting.
claims.evidence: Figma's editor-render unification is the canonical case; pre-Figma tools required round-trip exports.

terms.name: File of record
terms.notes: The canonical artifact a user returns to; not a derived preview.

turns.speaker: Mara Ito
turns.text: Welcome back to Files of Record. Today's question: why does the same team build something that feels permanent one year and something disposable the next?

turns.speaker: Vikram Shah
turns.text: It's almost always about whether the artifact survives the session. If you can come back to it next month and have it still mean something, it's an instrument. Otherwise it's a moment.

turns.speaker: Mara Ito
turns.text: So when you say 'apps vs instruments' — what's the actual fork in the road?

turns.speaker: Vikram Shah
turns.text: Apps optimize for the next session. Instruments optimize for the next year. When you compound use over time, the gap is enormous.

turns.speaker: Mara Ito
turns.text: Round-trip editing. You said earlier that's the threshold. Define it.

turns.speaker: Vikram Shah
turns.text: The output you produce has to be editable in the same surface where you produced it. No re-upload. No re-prompt. Otherwise your tool isn't an instrument — it's a generator.
